title: Highest temperature in London on July 25?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the London City Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 25 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the London City Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Official Met Office forecasts for London on July 25 project a maximum of 28°C under sunny intervals, aligning closely with the market's leading 28°C outcome at 35.5% implied probability. Ensemble guidance from models like ECMWF shows limited upside beyond 29°C due to moderating high pressure and a post-heatwave transition from earlier July peaks above 30°C. Typical late-July climatology hovers near 22–24°C, yet persistent warm anomalies and clear skies support the 27–29°C cluster holding over 75% combined probability. Updated model runs through July 22–23 have kept higher thresholds (31°C+) below 5% as Atlantic influences introduce modest cooling.

eventMetadata.context_description: Traders are positioning around 29–31°C for Paris’s July 25 maximum, reflecting the narrow spread typical of short-range ensemble forecasts two days ahead. Official guidance from Météo-France and global models (ECMWF, GFS) currently favors a mostly clear, high-pressure regime that supports afternoon temperatures near or slightly above the July climatological normal of ~25–27°C. Key differentiating factors include boundary-layer mixing depth, any late-day convective cloud development that caps insolation, and the precise timing of warm-air advection from the south. Minor model disagreements on cloud cover or wind speed can shift the expected peak by 1–2°C, producing the tight clustering around 29–30°C while keeping probabilities for 28°C or 32°C+ much lower. Updated runs and satellite observations over the next 24–48 hours will likely tighten or shift these odds as the diurnal heating profile becomes clearer.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Trader sentiment for Seoul's July 25 highest temperature reflects the tight clustering of forecasts around 28–32 °C amid typical midsummer East Asian monsoon conditions.** With 29 °C and 30 °C as the narrow leaders at 27.5 % and 27.0 % implied probability, markets price in modest day-to-day variability driven by short-term atmospheric details rather than large-scale regime shifts.

Current guidance from the Korea Meteorological Administration and ensemble models shows Seoul under a warm, moist southerly flow with surface highs near 30 °C. Key differentiating factors include afternoon cloud cover and shower timing, which modulate incoming solar radiation and evaporative cooling; boundary-layer humidity (often exceeding 80 %), which limits nighttime radiative cooling and keeps daytime maxima sensitive to even brief breaks in cloud; and urban heat-island effects that can add 1–2 °C in central districts on days with light winds. Recent model runs have converged on a 29–31 °C range, with small ensemble spreads reflecting uncertainty in the exact placement and intensity of any convective cells on the 25th. Historical July climatology places Seoul’s mean daily maximum near 29–30 °C, so the market distribution essentially captures the normal interannual and synoptic variability around that baseline. No major tropical cyclone or strong frontal passage is expected within the 48-hour window, keeping the outcome distribution narrow and responsive to the next official forecast updates.

title: Highest temperature in Singapore on July 25?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Singapore Changi Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 25 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Singapore Changi Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/sg/singapore/WSSS.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent southwest monsoon conditions over Singapore, with prevailing southeasterly winds and variable afternoon thundery showers, underpin the tight market-implied odds favoring 30°C or 31°C highs on July 25. These outcomes reflect trader assessment of typical July climatology—average daily maxima near 31°C—tempered by uncertainty over localized cloud cover and rainfall that can suppress peaks by 1–2°C. Drier intervals may allow brief exceedances toward 33°C, but official outlooks emphasize short-duration convection limiting sustained heat. Model consensus shows modest variability in steering patterns and humidity levels, with resolution hinging on precise observational maxima recorded by the National Environment Agency network.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent ECMWF and GFS ensemble runs position Milan’s July 25 maximum near 30–32 °C under persistent subtropical ridging, yet the leading 28 °C-or-below outcome (39.5 % implied) reflects model spread from an approaching Atlantic trough that could enhance northerly flow and reduce boundary-layer heating. Po Valley subsidence inversions and urban heat-island effects normally amplify daytime peaks, but recent soundings show slightly cooler mid-level air and higher low-level moisture that favor modest capping. Historical July maxima average 29–31 °C, so the tight clustering around 29–30 °C in market pricing captures the narrow uncertainty window before the next model cycle and observational updates from ARPA Lombardia.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Adolfo Suárez Madrid-Barajas Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 25 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Adolfo Suárez Madrid-Barajas Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/es/madrid/LEMD.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest model consensus from agencies including AEMET and ECMWF shows Madrid’s July 25 maximum settling near 29–30 °C under a weakening high-pressure ridge, with clear skies and light southwesterly flow limiting daytime heating after the mid-July heatwave. Recent runs indicate slightly cooler advection of maritime air and reduced insolation from increased boundary-layer moisture, narrowing the spread between 28 °C and 31 °C outcomes. Key differentiators include precise timing of any frontal passage, urban heat-island amplification, and model resolution of local wind patterns that can add or subtract 1–2 °C at observation sites. Updated 00Z and 12Z model cycles plus official AEMET briefings on July 24 will refine these probabilities ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent ensemble forecasts from ECMWF and national meteorological services indicate maximum temperatures in Warsaw on July 25 most likely in the 24–26°C range, with the market's tight clustering around 25°C reflecting genuine model spread and limited differentiation among runs. Key variables include the strength and timing of any Atlantic trough, potential for increased cloud cover or scattered showers that could cap daytime heating, and modest variations in boundary-layer moisture and wind. Historical July averages near 24–25°C provide context, while the absence of strong high-pressure dominance or heat advection keeps probabilities for extremes low. Updated model guidance and official IMGW briefings in the next 48 hours will refine resolution criteria for the daily high.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent short-range forecasts from the Finnish Meteorological Institute and European models point to a daily maximum near 20°C in Helsinki on July 25, with 19–21°C outcomes holding the bulk of market-implied probability due to modest ensemble spread. Key drivers include variable low-level cloud cover reducing insolation, weak southerly advection of slightly warmer Baltic air, and the potential timing of a weak frontal passage that could cap or boost afternoon temperatures by 1–2°C. July climatology places the average high near 21°C, so current conditions sit close to seasonal norms while model disagreement on boundary-layer mixing and radiative effects sustains the tight clustering around the central bins. Updated runs from national weather services in the next 48 hours will likely narrow the distribution ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent southwest monsoon conditions over Peninsular Malaysia, combined with limited cloud cover and typical July insolation, underpin the tight clustering of market-implied probabilities around 30–32 °C for Kuala Lumpur’s July 25 maximum. Daytime heating under the monsoon regime often produces afternoon peaks near the 32 °C climatological average, yet scattered convective showers or increased boundary-layer moisture can readily suppress the daily high by 1–2 °C. The July 16 reading of 34 °C illustrates how brief clear-sky episodes can push temperatures higher, keeping the 33 °C+ tail at 11 %. Short-range model consensus from Malaysian Meteorological Department guidance shows modest day-to-day variability in cloud timing and wind, explaining why no single outcome exceeds 28 % and why traders price the 30–32 °C band so closely. Updated forecasts and surface observations through July 24 will provide the next resolution-relevant data.

eventMetadata.context_description: Traders are pricing the highest temperature in Guangzhou on July 25 around 36–39 °C because official forecasts from sources such as timeanddate and local observatories converge on a daytime high near 37 °C under partly cloudy skies with possible thunderstorms. In Guangzhou’s subtropical monsoon climate, peak July heat stems from strong solar insolation, high humidity that limits nocturnal cooling, and the urban heat-island effect; however, afternoon convective clouds or showers can shave 1–2 °C off maximum readings while clear intervals allow brief spikes toward 39 °C. Model consensus shows limited day-to-day variability this close to the date, keeping the distribution tight and explaining why lower or extreme outcomes (below 35 °C or 40 °C+) carry minimal implied probability.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader sentiment for Manila's July 25 high temperature centers on the southwest monsoon (Habagat) and associated cloud cover, which limit daytime heating and favor maxima of 30–31°C. PAGASA forecasts indicate 30–31°C highs with widespread showers and thunderstorms, consistent with seasonal wet-period patterns where convective activity and high humidity cap peaks near the climatological average of 31°C. Model consensus shows limited potential for clearer skies that could allow brief warming to 32°C or higher, while heavy rainfall and easterly flow make lower outcomes around 29°C plausible if convection intensifies. The near-even split between the top two brackets reflects forecast uncertainty in monsoon strength and timing of any breaks in cloud cover over the next 48 hours.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Trader sentiment for Lucknow’s July 25 maximum temperature centers on the interplay between active southwest monsoon conditions and short-term forecast uncertainty.** As of July 23, recent IMD observations show daily highs near 33°C with light rain or thundershowers, consistent with the monsoon trough remaining near its normal position and delivering isolated rainfall over Uttar Pradesh. Below-normal July rainfall forecasts from IMD models imply reduced cloud cover on some days, supporting slightly higher maxima, while any increase in convective activity or heavier showers would suppress peaks through enhanced evaporative cooling and reduced solar insolation.

Forecasts from sources such as NDTV and AccuWeather cluster around 33–34°C for July 25, with IMD guidance indicating partly cloudy skies and light rain that typically caps highs in the low-to-mid 30s. The near-even market-implied odds for 32°C versus 33°C reflect model divergence on rainfall timing and intensity over the next 48 hours, as even modest differences in cloud cover or localized downpours can shift the daily maximum by 1–2°C. Historical July climatology (average highs near 32–34°C) and current above-normal minimum temperatures further anchor expectations in this narrow range. Updated IMD district forecasts and high-resolution model runs expected in the coming day will likely refine the outcome ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Trader sentiment for Cape Town’s July 25 maximum temperature reflects tight clustering around 19–21 °C, driven by short-range model consensus for mostly fine, partly sunny conditions following an approaching cold front.** South African Weather Service guidance for 24–25 July indicates clearing skies and temperatures that are cool to locally warm, while several operational forecasts explicitly project an afternoon high near 20 °C under light winds and minimal cloud cover.  

Cape Town’s winter maxima are strongly modulated by the cold Benguela Current along the west coast, which caps daytime warming, and by the strength and direction of the southeasterly “Cape Doctor” winds that enhance mixing and limit thermal buildup. With the front expected to clear by the 25th, reduced cloud cover and subsidence should allow modest radiational warming; however, small differences in predicted wind speed, residual moisture, or the precise timing of high pressure building can shift the peak by 1–2 °C. Historical July averages hover near 17–18 °C, so the current market-implied range already incorporates a mild positive anomaly consistent with recent broader winter patterns.  

Model runs (GFS/ECMWF) and SAWS updates over the next 24–48 hours will be the key catalysts, as even minor revisions to cloud or wind fields can reallocate probability among the closely bunched 19–21 °C outcomes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent frontal systems and associated cloud cover over southeastern Brazil have suppressed daytime heating in São Paulo ahead of July 25, keeping maximum temperatures near or below seasonal July averages of 22°C. Official model guidance diverges on the timing of any clearing: persistent low-level moisture and light southerly flow favor highs of 20–21°C if skies remain mostly overcast, while partial breaks in cloudiness could allow greater insolation and push readings to 22°C. Urban heat-island effects at official stations add minor variability, but the narrow spread in market-implied odds (centered on 20–22°C) reflects genuine forecast uncertainty in boundary-layer mixing and precipitation timing rather than any single dominant driver. Updated model runs and INMET observations tomorrow morning will likely resolve the leading outcomes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecast models from the National Weather Service and AccuWeather indicate a high temperature near 84–85°F for Atlanta on July 25, driven by partly cloudy skies, light westerly winds, and scattered thunderstorm chances that may limit peak heating. eventMetadata.context_description: National Weather Service point forecasts and ensemble model guidance for Miami International Airport project a July 25 high near 91–92 °F under typical mid-summer subtropical ridging, anchoring the tight 45.5 % and 40 % implied probabilities on the 92–93 °F and 90–91 °F bins. Abundant low-level moisture and a modest sea-breeze circulation favor scattered afternoon convection that caps extreme heating while also limiting how far temperatures can fall below guidance. With only modest spread among short-range models and no strong signals for drier mid-level air or delayed thunderstorm initiation, traders see limited room for 94 °F-plus outcomes (9 %) or sub-90 °F results (under 9 % combined). The next 12Z NWS update and morning sounding data will be the key near-term catalysts.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Current forecasts from the National Weather Service and AccuWeather place Austin’s July 25 high temperature in the mid-to-upper 90s°F, aligning closely with the market’s leading outcomes around 94–97°F (combined ~58% implied probability).** 

July climatology supports this range, with a normal daily high of 96.6°F and a seasonal peak near 99–100°F by month’s end, driven by persistent subtropical high pressure promoting strong daytime heating, low relative humidity, and minimal cloud cover. Recent observations show the region under a heat dome that delivered the year’s highest temperatures so far in the mid-to-upper 90s through July 21–23, with only isolated convection and no significant frontal passage expected to cool the boundary layer before the 25th. Model consensus points to light southeasterly flow and surface dewpoints in the low-to-mid 70s, conditions that favor efficient radiational heating without widespread thunderstorms that could cap maximums. Traders appear to be weighting these stable synoptic patterns heavily, while assigning lower odds to 100°F+ outcomes that would require an unusually strong downslope or drier air mass not currently indicated in guidance. Updated NWS and model runs issued in the next 24 hours will be the key near-term catalyst for any probability shifts.

**Recent National Weather Service guidance and private model consensus indicate a return to hot, mostly sunny conditions across northeast Colorado for July 25, 2026, following mid-week thunderstorm activity, positioning the highest temperature near 98–101 °F as the leading outcomes.**

Persistent high pressure and dry adiabatic warming from downslope flow off the Rockies, combined with strong July insolation at Denver’s 5,280-foot elevation, favor rapid daytime heating under mostly clear skies. Ensemble forecasts show afternoon highs climbing into the upper 90s to low 100s, with limited moisture and light winds reducing evaporative cooling. Market-implied probabilities concentrate in the 98–101 °F bins because current model runs show little spread around this range, though slight variations in cloud cover or boundary-layer mixing could shift the peak by a degree or two. eventMetadata.context_description: Traders are heavily favoring 94-95°F (44% implied probability) as the peak temperature in Houston on July 25, 2026, followed by 92-93°F (27%), reflecting consensus from National Weather Service and model guidance showing typical midsummer conditions under a building subtropical ridge. Normal highs for late July at Houston Intercontinental Airport average 94-96°F, driven by strong insolation, southerly flow off the Gulf of Mexico, and minimal cloud cover early in the day. Recent developments include an expanding heat dome across Texas that has produced highs in the mid-90s to near 100°F in prior days, with limited afternoon convection expected to provide slight cooling without widespread rain. Model runs show low precipitation chances and dew points in the mid-70s, supporting heat indices above 105°F but limiting extreme outliers. Any late-day sea breeze or scattered storms could cap readings near the lower end of the favored range, while stronger subsidence would push toward 96-97°F.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Trader consensus for Panama City, Panama’s July 25 high temperature centers on 31–33 °C because climatological averages and short-range models place the daily maximum in that narrow band.** July is peak rainy-season conditions, with the Intertropical Convergence Zone driving frequent afternoon convection that limits peak heating through cloud cover and evaporative cooling. Recent forecasts from sources such as AccuWeather and local models show expected highs of 29–31 °C under partly cloudy skies with isolated showers, keeping the most probable outcomes tightly clustered.

The near-even split between 32 °C and 33 °C (30.5 % and 29.0 %) reflects uncertainty in the timing and intensity of any convective cells on the 25th; earlier or heavier rainfall would cap the maximum closer to 31 °C, while a later or weaker shower pattern could allow brief clearing and a push toward 33 °C. Lower-probability 34 °C+ outcomes would require unusually suppressed convection and stronger insolation, conditions not currently favored by guidance. Historical July maxima average near 30–31 °C, so market pricing aligns with both the seasonal baseline and the latest model consensus that modest variability around that mean is the most likely scenario.
